speaker: interviewee
text: --people in one barracks. But we-- the, the camp, Camp C, had about 30,000 people, 30 barracks for the people, then was one for the kitchen, one, the latrine, one for storage, and then the rest for the Germans, and so on. The--

speaker: interviewer
text: So let me understand. In one barrack, there were how many people?

speaker: interviewee
text: About a thousand.

speaker: interviewer
text: In one barrack, there were a thousand people?

speaker: interviewee
text: A thousand people. It was a long barrack, a big one.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there was only one woman?

speaker: interviewee
text: One worker just. And she had some helpers, kapos, and other people who have--

speaker: interviewer
text: What is a kapo?

speaker: interviewee
text: A-- kapos also took care of the-- of the-- of the people. When we went, she was, like, under the Blockalteste.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you have liked being a kapo?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a thought that I always said to myself. I wish to have a position for one reason-- not so much to have more food, even though this was the most important thing, but I wanted to try out myself. Would I have been good to people, to help them? Because some people, they're good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or would I have been like Itel? But I didn't ever had a chance to find out. But I had this thought many, many times. What would you have had to do to become a kapo?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. You had to have somebody who knows you. You know, like a sister who is a Blockalteste, she will-- or, or if you-- I don't know. I have no idea how you could get it. But I had no way to, to become one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me some stories about the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: I would like to tell you about the first few nights that we had three-level beds. And the beds were-- I never know the measurements-- not like, you know, today, like, 6 by, by 10 or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: A single bed?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: A double bed?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, much-- like, like three double beds, large, three levels. But 15 people, they're on one level. And we had room only to lie on one side. And if one person want to turn around, everybody had to turn around. And in the middle of the night, somebody had to turn around because our bones started to ache. It was the wooden planks. And we use the shoes for-- we took off the shoes. They went up because-- not to hit somebody. And also, took-- this is what we used for pillows, our shoes. No covers, anything, in Auschwitz, so-- but it was the summertime. And night was quite cold. But if one person want to turn around, everybody had to turn around. And not everybody wanted to turn around. So it was all this shouting and screaming. And very often, upper bunk would broke-- break down and fall on the lower people. And people got hurt. And then the Blockalteste would come out and scream at us. But she came out and brought a hammer and nails. And somebody had to fix it and to go back.

speaker: interviewer
text: The planks broke.

speaker: interviewee
text: The planks, yes. But in the first night-- I don't know exactly if it was a week or two, or less-- even without breaking down the bunk beds, somebody will start to scream, oh, they're going to gas us. And then a hysteria broke out. All 1,000 people will start to scream from the bottom of their heart, just to scream in fear. It was such a horrible noise that when it started, I used to hold onto my mother and my sister and say, we're not going to scream. Let's not scream. I said, if we won't scream, it, it will be better. And then, suddenly-- and also, it happened sometimes outside, when we were outside in the camp. Because sometimes-- planes will go around, and they will spray the camp. They always sprayed us with something, from the camp on our bodies.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the spray looked like some haze. So somebody would say, oh, they're gassing us. And it was enough to start to, to scream and to shout.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I heard people talking like this, I used to walk away from them because I got scared. And I didn't want to be scared. Because I felt, honestly, if will be scared, I will die. And I didn't want to die.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one day, it stopped, the screaming. And at that point, we already asked around from people who were there longer, what happened? Nobody's screaming anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: [DRINKS] And then we found out that they put bromide in the food, the little food they gave us-- bromide, a tranquilizer. And not only it calmed down the people-- because the Germans didn't want us to scream. They liked us to be like animals, the way they wanted us to be. So-- and also took care of the menstruations of all the women. And this was a blessing because I didn't have anything for it. But after the war, when we started to eat normally, we're back, everything turned back to normal. So this was the screaming. The latrine was a, a--

speaker: interviewer
text: So, wait. Excuse me. So the, the reason people stopped screaming is because they got bromide?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of the tranquilizer, yes. Otherwise, it wouldn't have stopped.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I always think is-- today, when they look for birth control, why they don't choose bromide? Maybe it would be so much simpler to, to do it. The latrine-- because this affected me some until today. The latrine was one of the big barracks and had three cement rows with hundreds of holes in it. And hundreds of holes sounds so much. But to think that, at the same time, 30,000 women were allowed to go to the latrine in the middle of the night, it was an experience for itself-- shouting, pushing, screaming. And if you were lucky enough to find a hole, very often it was dirty. There's not much you could do about it. And in the walls of the latrine, you had some water faucets. Again, if you can picture thousands of people trying to get to it at the same time. So all life, everything, was always pushing, shoving, screaming. I remember once, when I wasn't in, in the group. And somebody pushed probably a mile away, but it came ahead. And a woman turned around. And she started to scream at me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why are you pushing? You're probably from Maramaros. Maramaros was a place where I come from that wasn't so elegant as Budapest. So even there, you could feel. So she screamed at me. I was from Maramaros, but she didn't know it. But she accused me of being, like, when they said, you are probably a Jew. This is why you do it. I said, why are you screaming at me? I am pushed, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the people were so edgy. It didn't really matter what you did or who you are. But everything little thing, you burst out. So I wasn't really angry at the woman that she called me names or whatever. But this was life, how we did it. The food in Auschwitz, the lunch-- every day, and I was there from May until beginning of October-- every day when it was lunchtime, I always told myself, after the war-- because I believed there will be after the war-- they will be able to make a two-hour movie and call it Because it was an experience to watch. 30,000 women they let outside to stay in lines, in hundreds of lines, to get the soup. Morning, we didn't receive anything. Lunchtime, we received a vegetable soup that was watery. And you could feel the sand in it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the first few days, we didn't even eat it. But then we learned that-- we have to remind ourself we cannot run back to the kitchen and grab a piece of bread or anything we want because I don't like the soup. So later, we were praying to have a little bit more of this bad soup.

speaker: interviewee
text: [DRINKS] And in this soup, probably, they put the bromides. What happened-- the girls who were lucky enough to work in the kitchen, they brought out the kettles of the hot soup open. And some people, when you got hold of something, it was called 'organisieren.' Only if you took away from another prisoner, it was called stealing. But if you could go through the storage room and grab a piece of a dish or a piece of clothing, it was no sin. And it was called 'organisieren.' So some people organized for themselves a little dish with a handle. And this was, like, lifesaving, because later I, I did something with a dish, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when those girls came with the soup, they would run. It was really a risk. And they would run to the-- to the kettle, and scoop up soup, and run away, and have some extra food.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it was so risky, because even the-- all Germans or the Blockaltestes could see you. And they will beat you up if they get you. But people were so hungry, they didn't think of the consequences. They just did it. And I, somewhere, even though I was quite brave in circumstance, I never dared to do this. And I didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then the people received the soup. And while we're standing in the line, will I get the top of the kettle? Or will I get the bottom? Because sometime the girls didn't bother to mix it up. In the bottom, you had more vegetables than on the top. So the excitement was always there. Every bite counted.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we sat on the ground. And we received those military canteens. And we got a scoop of soup. We sat down, eating. And there was a SS man, at least where I was. There were probably many. But I always talk about what was around me. And he was walking around-- and with his mocking smile on his face. And he, like, I always could read his face, like let's see what, what those hungry Jews will do. He wanted to see what a hungry person will risk. And he will walk around, and suddenly call up a woman who was sitting. And he said, you took twice the soup, and beat her up. And we watched, and we got scared. Another time, he will get out somebody from the line and beat her up. You took twice.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it took us some times to figure out how did he know that she took twice? So we figured it out. And this is what I-- what I met before, we learned a lot of things while we were there. But in the beginning, we didn't know it.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was watching. Some were standing in line with a dish that was used already. Then he knew, oh, she takes another helping because the dish wasn't clean. Or then he saw somebody sitting and eating and next to her was another dish that was used already. This was enough to beat her up.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would it get clean after food?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, somebody cleaned it in the kitchen. There--

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: --were people who worked in the kitchen.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't have water to clean it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, so you didn't keep the dishes.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they collect them.

speaker: interviewer
text: You only get-- I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had to give it back. But if he saw somebody sitting there, let's say, three, four person, there was an extra dish-- he will pick up the girl and beat her up. And the same thing happened when we stood Appell. In the afternoon, we had to get out when it was real hot. And as I mentioned before, in the middle of the night, they took us out. And we had to stand up when it was still very dark and cold, twice a day. Only Sundays we stood once. And suddenly, we discovered what hair is. It's not only for beauty because it protects us from heat and from cold. At night, we were so cold in our heads because we didn't have hair. And if somebody was lucky enough to have a big dress, we tore off a piece of the dress and put it on our head. And in daytime, the sun was so high that you got sunburn in your head. So this is how we learned what hair is. And some people had diarrhea. A lot of people were sick. So when-- daytime, we had to stand hours to line up five people in a line, 500 people in a group, until the Germans come to count us. So some people sat down. And we had them, weak people, sick people. And when the SS came in to count us-- a whole group came. And they went to every barracks, one of them. And the, the counting took minutes, because 5, 10, 15, it went very fast. But we had to stand hours before. So when they start to come, the news came, they're coming. So everybody straightened up.

speaker: interviewer
text: And again, this one SS man who came to our group, before he count us, he went around and looked and looked on everybody's back, picked out the girl-- you were sitting down-- and beat her up. Again, we had to figure out how did he know? It took us several days.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we realized that he looked at our dresses. And if somebody had dust on it, then he knew that was sat down. So from, from that day on, if somebody was sitting-- they didn't stop sitting who had it-- I sat down many times, too. But we cleaned our dress. So we had somebody else to clean it real up so avoid the punishment.

speaker: interviewer
text: [DRINKS] You--

speaker: interviewee
text: In the morning when we stood Appell, when it was pitch dark and cold-- we were so cold. The only way to, to warm us, we stood close to each other. And we warmed each other with our bodies. But there was the problem-- five people. We didn't dare to do more people because they can come any minute. The, the person in front was cold in the front. And the person in the back, the back. So then again, it was a whole shuffle and screaming. Everybody tried to be in the middle. And my mother used to say, I will stand in the end or the front. Just let my girls be in the middle. And she was so fragile. And she was a small woman, and she wasn't a healthy woman. But obviously, she became strong. And one day, I decided I'm going to stand in the front or in the back to hide myself. What if one day-- because the fear was constant that they will separate me from my mother, not from my sister, but from my mother. I had this fear all the time. And I volunteered to stand in the front. And I was so miserably cold that next day, I pushed myself again in the middle. I said, I have time to, to strain myself when I will have to. And I told this my children. And my older daughter once used it against me, Esther, because I told her. And she said, I have time to do it when I will have to. You did it in Auschwitz. I have time to do it, too. So it's a lesson, in a way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me, did you ever have diarrhea?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember diarrhea. But I remember when we were inside in the-- in the barracks-- the end of the barracks was a little corner with buckets for toilet use. But we couldn't use it all the time. There were certain hours when I, too, or the other block women, they decided to let us go. I think I had diarrhea. But it's so long ago. I just remember that going to the latrine was a-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] an experience. And then in inside and people had diarrhea. And we had to stand in line. She was always telling us, if you will be quiet, you can go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So finally she said, OK, you can go. So we had to line up. There were four or five buckets for a thousand people. So we lined up. And it didn't go fast. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And some people dirtied themselves, said they couldn't wait for the line. Then she will come out and beat her up.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when it happened to me, I had to go so badly. And my sister was standing with me, Lilly. My mother wasn't there. She was on the bunk bed. And I suddenly said, Lilly, I cannot hold anymore. And I have to go. She said, no, you cannot, because she will beat you up. And I felt-- it wasn't diarrhea. Just to go out-- and I had to hold back. And suddenly I got such pain-- this was the only time. I never felt-- I didn't know you can have such terrible pain, but not able to go to the bathroom-- to-- just to urine, not diarrhea. And she would hold me back, my sister. Her-- don't, don't. And we're almost there. And she talked to me until we arrived. And I succeed to reach the buckets and not to be punished.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have any repercussions from this having to hold back?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think so, because I suffer until today from chronic constipation. And I tried every remedy under the sun. It started right after the war, before I was married. My mother used to bring me the water from-- when you make cheese, cheese water, because this is good. Later, when I met my husband-- used to make me every morning on my empty stomach a glass of grapefruit juice, all kind of remedies. And it helped. Every remedy helps me for once or twice. I have several teas and several tranquilizers. And if I take the-- not tranquilizers, laxatives. And if I take the same laxative three times in a row, it doesn't work anymore, like my stomach says, you are not going to cheat me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it's a constant maneuvering for myself. I'm managing it, because my doctor always gives me-- I said, doctor, I am managing it. Because when I got for a check-up, ask me how are you, I said, my constipation's still active. But I am handling it. So I think this is one of the leftovers that I have from this experience that I had. So you, you managed it then. And you're managing it now.

speaker: interviewee
text: [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: But you're still in control.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it hard to be in control? Not only in the bathroom business, but in control of your feelings?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't if it was hard. But I somehow always found a way to be in control. For instance, I remember one day, we were walking outside. And everybody was over 25 at that time-- I thought as an older lady, an older woman.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I heard two women talking, you don't know where we are. We'll never get out of here. You see the chimneys? They won't let us out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I got scared. And every time I was there for that-- so many months, every time, when I looked at those chimneys, they were active. All the time while I was in Auschwitz, I could see all flames coming out or smoke. But I can't recall looking-- and they were so high, that no matter where you were, you could see them. I never recall looking at them-- and they should be just buildings. And I got so scared when those two women said that we will never get out of it, that I ran away. And I decided I am not going to listen to people who are so pessimistic. I am just running away. Then another time, we were divided from other camps. And one side of the camps were men. And one-- and we use to come and to look for relatives. So we used to come and to talk to each other to ask, maybe you are with somebody from my town, from my family, and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one day, there were standing two teenagers-- on one side, a girl, the other side, a boy. And they were from the same hometown. They were very glad to see each other. And the girl asked the boy, who is left from your family? And he looked at her, and put a big smile on his face, said, only two of us-- me and my good humor.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was so happy to hear that, I remember it until today. He kept his good humor. And this is what I did the rest of my life. I ran away from pessimists, and I only wanted to stick to optimists. I actually became one. Because later, when I was working in my other camp, where I worked-- in the factory, we could mingle with other groups and with the men. And I use to go away from-- for a little rest. I used to go to the men. And they always liked me to come. Said, oh, Edith, come here. And you, you have always a smile. And we used to talk and to try to forget the misery. So I think I-- this is how I controlled my-- I always found something. For instance, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- if I will live, I don't know how long, people will ask me what was the hardest thing to endure, I will always say the constant, constant hunger. The hard work, the cold, everything else was terrible. But the constant hunger was something that it's impossible to describe. In a whole year, there wasn't one instance-- even though I learned later to organize myself food. But I shared my food with my mother and my sister evenly. I could have keep it for myself and be full. Not once did I have enough food to say I'm not hungry for a whole year. And I had made up for myself two prayers. One prayer, I said, if I have to die, I pray to God, please let me die today rather than tomorrow, so I will be one day less hungry. And the other prayer was, if I have to die, please, God, let me once, once more in my life have enough food. And then I can die. This is how bad the hunger was. I cannot give a better example. But I remember those feelings, how I felt.

speaker: interviewer
text: [DRINKS] What happened to you after Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, in Auschwitz, one-- Mengele used to come in for selections. And the fear was always tremendous when he came in because we never knew what he will select people for. If you look for some young people who looked still good, and he picked this group, and the other stayed in the camp, we knew this group will go away to work somewhere. And without knowing what is outside, I always felt that any place is better than Auschwitz. And I was right. Now I can say it. But I felt it at that time. If he picked some people who are very skinny or people who hardly could work, we knew that he has extra room in the gas chambers. And he wants to fill them up. This is why he's looking for those people. And the third fear was, even if he will look for good-- for people for life, for work, the fear was always he will separate mother and us, or sisters and sisters. And many times, we succeeded. He always separated mother from us. But we always succeeded, in the back of the huge group, to run back to her. And one day, when we went through the same procedure, Lilly and I on this side, and mother, the other side, Lilly was the first one to run back. And when I was running back, a SS saw me. And he looked at me. And he pointed at his rifle. He said, you better watch out. And I got scared, and I stayed separated. So I was separated from mother and Lilly. But they didn't take me out of Auschwitz. They took me to another camp. And the other camp was Camp B, that-- previously, it, it was the camp-- all the people of Theresienstadt who were brought from Theresienstadt to Auschwitz with families, they were in this camp. And I used to watch how those people, they're taking away the young women, the young men. And I used to see how they used to walk. And we could see them far away. And they used to wave to each other, the rest of the families did. And it made them always cry so hardly. And since then, I don't like goodbyes, because it always reminds me how those people left, the young women, the young men. Because later, all those people who were left, the older people, they were sent to the gas chambers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when we travel somewhere or somehow, I don't like to say goodbyes because it reminds me of this. Who knows, will we see each other again? And I was-- they emptied this camp. And then people from Camp C who were picked were taken to Camp B.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there, the next day, I was called. I'm Camp B. So I could come to the-- to the wires. And I could see mother and Lilly. And we used to come to each other and talk to each other. And there, I got my tattoo number, in Camp B. And only-- I learned recently that only in Auschwitz they tattooed the numbers. And in Auschwitz, not everybody, because my mother didn't have a number. My sister doesn't have a number. And most of my relatives and friends here are survivors. And only those who were in Camp B have the numbers. They didn't make it in Camp C.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your number? Can you show me?

speaker: interviewee
text: My number-- I cannot-- I don't-- I'm not, never-- I have ever showed now, ever. I knew it by heart, but not anymore. I don't have to remember it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't look at it very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: My number is A-- I don't know what A stands for-- 13215. And when they made the number, they connected this number to my name. We received a number in Camp B. We had to stand in line in alphabetical order-- the only time when they ask our names. And there was a-- we didn't know, first, why we were standing in line. And then we realized when we always stood in line-- and we approached something, so when we came closer, we could see what's happening. And when I saw this, I got scared. I said, oh my god. They took away my family, my freedom, my hair, my clothes, my everything. And now they're going to put a number like I'm cattle. And it happened to be August the 18th. Somehow we knew the dates. It was my 17th birthday. And I was thinking, I said, this birthday, I will remember. Not only that I was in Auschwitz, but that I got my number. And by, by saying I will remember, it gave me hope that I will survive. So I-- because if you die, you don't remember anything. So this gave me hope again. And then the other thought came to me. I said to myself, if they make a number, they're not going to send me to the gas chambers. They will send me-- but that, that point, I wanted to leave Auschwitz very much. They will send me away to work. And then I will have a chance.

speaker: interviewer
text: So by the time I-- my turn came, and I told them my name and my country, and I stretched out my arm, I didn't even feel the pain. Because it gave me so much hope, my figuring out-- somehow, I always tried to find something positive. And this, this kept me going. This was one way of going.

speaker: interviewee
text: And not long after this-- yeah, this was in August. No, I still was there September-- about five more weeks, when again, Mengele came in. And he selected again a group of people for work.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know they selected him for work?

speaker: interviewee
text: We could feel it. Because we could see which type of people he's picking.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: He picked young girls who still looked healthy. He picked 400 young women. But what happened to us, again, we were taken away. We were taken to delousing building. And there, we went through the shower again. They didn't shave our hair again. We had already short hair. They give us clean clothes. And they gave us a coat. Now, this was another sign. It was October, and they gave us a coat-- so that we were going somewhere. Then we came and we had to, to board the train, again, cattle train, not a regular train. And while we were outside, a commotion. First, several things happened to me on this day, [SIGHS] two very painful things.

speaker: interviewer
text: One of the most painful things happened to me that it's maybe the second or the third time I'm talking about it. It's just too much for me-- so the saddest experience in my life, in a way. when I was marching to the main gate to leave Auschwitz, the only thought I had was, how will I let mother and Lilly know that I'm taken away?

speaker: interviewee
text: [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: There is no way. And while I was marching, suddenly, from the side of my eye, I felt somebody running parallel with me on the other side of the wire. And it took a few seconds until I turn my head. And it was my mother. It was like a miracle. How did she know that I'm going away?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I said to mother, Mother, [NON-ENGLISH], I'm going away. And I don't remember what we talked. But I was-- I had to walk. And she was running parallel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then suddenly, it hit me. I said, where is Lilly? And she said, Lilly was taken away yesterday. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It would be better for me if I wouldn't known that Lilly was taken away because then I would believe until the end of the war that she was with Lilly.

speaker: interviewee
text: But that she told me this, I felt-- I thought, that's the end of my mother. And whenever I thought about it since, I was crying so much, I think I don't have tears left. The rest of, of the year, whenever I thought about my mother, I was crying, because I had this picture, my little fragile mother, skinny, running parallel to me, saying goodbye. And a goodbye in Auschwitz meant forever. And after the war, when I heard that my mother is alive, people argued with me. I didn't believe it. I thought, they are setting me up. And until I didn't see her, I didn't believe it. And some-- by some miracle, she was chosen with my aunt, whom I mentioned before, and a cousin of hers, the three older ladies, they were taken away to another camp. I don't remember the name. She told me once. But she was liberated in January '45. She wouldn't have lasted till May, like I did and my sister. So-- and we heard that my brother was separate from my father. So all five of us, we were separated. We didn't know about each other. And when I came back, when I found my mother, the next day, my sister came back. But the funny part about this is that very few people of the survivors can talk about it, including my sister. And I will tell you later how, how I can prove this. I have a proof how little we talk about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: About what?

speaker: interviewee
text: About our being in concentration camp, when we were separated.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: About our own experiences, we don't talk too much--

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't talk about--

speaker: interviewee
text: --with each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because it's too painful. People don't like to talk about it. This was one thing-- when I was liberated, I told myself, I am not going to talk about it ever. I'm not going to think about it. I'm going to build a new life for myself. And then coincidences forced me to talk about it.
